chapter_summary: Decoding is a necessary but insufficient foundation for reading; while schools have improved at teaching students to sound out words, they have failed to address the knowledge deficit that prevents comprehension. To achieve universal reading proficiency, schools must move beyond 'process-oriented' instruction and systematically build the background knowledge required to overcome the 'Matthew Effect' in literacy.

claims.text: Schools have recently become significantly better at teaching children decoding, the process of turning printed symbols into sounds and words accurately.
claims.source_passage: Recently schools have begun to do a much better job of teaching all children to become good first-step readers who can turn printed symbols into sounds and words quickly and accurately, a process called decoding.

claims.text: The notion that decoding is a natural process akin to learning to speak is a thoroughly incorrect idea.
claims.source_passage: the romantic notion that decoding is best regarded as a natural process, in the same way children learn to speak. Such an approach is a thoroughly incorrect idea that has in the past left many children by the wayside.

claims.text: The most effective and fastest method for teaching decoding is through persistent, explicit instruction starting no later than kindergarten.
claims.source_passage: These researchers have shown that the best and fastest way to teach decoding is through persistent, explicit instruction, a little at a time, starting no later than kindergarten.

claims.text: Achieving skill in decoding does not guarantee that a student will become a skilled reader.
claims.source_passage: But becoming a skilled decoder does not insure that one will become a skilled reader.

claims.text: The assumption that reading comprehension follows automatically from decoding fluency is false for the majority of students.
claims.source_passage: the present reality has not conformed to the old assumption that good comprehension follows automatically from gaining a good early start in decoding.

claims.text: Fewer than half of American students currently reach proficiency in reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: But since fewer than half our students actually reach proficiency in reading comprehension, the present reality has not conformed to the old assumption.

claims.text: A reader must typically understand approximately 90 percent of the words in a passage to comprehend the remaining 10 percent.
claims.source_passage: Many specialists estimate that a child or an adult needs to understand around 90 percent of the words in a passage in order to learn to understand the other 10 percent of the words.

claims.text: Reading comprehension requires an understanding of the specific 'referred-to realities' or the concrete subject matter the words represent.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, it’s not just the words that the student has to grasp the meaning of; it’s also the kind of reality that the words are referring to.

claims.text: The 'Matthew Effect' applies to literacy: children with high initial language understanding gain proficiency faster, while those without it fall further behind.
claims.source_passage: reading researchers have spoken of the “Matthew effect” in reading. Those who already have good language understanding will gain still more language proficiency, while those who lack initial understanding will fall further and further behind.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is primarily a problem of background knowledge rather than a lack of formal skills.
claims.source_passage: We need to see the reading comprehension problem for what it primarily is—a knowledge problem.

claims.text: Reading is incorrectly conceived in modern schools as a special, separate skill that transfers automatically between different texts.
claims.source_passage: reading is misconceived as a special, separate skill that, once learned, will grow automatically and be transferred from one text to another.

claims.text: Narrowing the achievement gap requires schools to systematically build concrete knowledge in all students starting in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: Schooling will need to pay close and systematic attention to the concrete knowledge that all students are gradually acquiring, so that each early gain will lead to later ones. This will not only raise general academic achievement... it will also narrow the large academic achievement gap.

claims.text: Once children learn to decode accurately and fluently, the primary reason for poor reading is a lack of knowledge about the subjects the words refer to.
claims.source_passage: Once children learn how to decode the printed word accurately and fluently, the main reason they do not read as well as they should is that they do not know as much as they should about the various things the printed words refer to.

claims.text: There is no fundamental distinction between the processing of oral and written speech.
claims.source_passage: The superficial differences between writing and talking must not mislead us into making a fundamental distinction between oral and written speech.

claims.text: The early years (ages four through seven) are the most crucial for gaining a start in language development.
claims.source_passage: The crucial years for gaining a good start in language are the early years.

claims.text: American schools have neglected oral language enhancement in the early years (ages four through seven) since reading began to be viewed as a separate skill.
claims.source_passage: In recent decades, since reading has been misconceived as a separate skill, there has been a relative neglect of enhancing oral language in the early years of schooling (ages four through seven).

claims.text: Early verbal and world knowledge acts cumulatively, similar to an interest-bearing bank account.
claims.source_passage: A good early start in verbal knowledge and world knowledge leads children to accrue still more knowledge each subsequent year, as in an interest-bearing bank account.

claims.text: Between ages two and seven, language progress occurs primarily through listening and talking rather than reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: At the youngest ages, two through seven, long before children can read as well as they can listen, progress in language occurs chiefly through listening and talking, not through reading and writing.

claims.text: Schools spend an excessive amount of time on simple written materials that young children can already understand for themselves.
claims.source_passage: we have tended to spend large amounts of time—too much time—on the simple cat-in-the-hat kinds of written material that young children are able to read and understand for themselves.

claims.text: The process of decoding printed marks into words is so taxing for first and second graders that it leaves little mental attention for understanding complex ideas.
claims.source_passage: the child’s laborious process of turning the printed marks into words and sentences is so taxing that he or she has little attention left for understanding anything but simple ideas.

claims.text: Simple self-reading materials typically used in early grades are far below the actual comprehension potential of young children.
claims.source_passage: this often trivial material is far below the comprehension potential of young children from all walks of life.

claims.text: Failing to read aloud and discuss challenging material with children results in a missed opportunity to build decisive world knowledge.
claims.source_passage: If we do not spend large amounts of time reading aloud and discussing challenging material with children—material that is well beyond their ability to decode with understanding—we miss a critical opportunity to increase their knowledge of language and of the world—the kind of knowledge that will prove decisive for reading in later years.

claims.text: The lack of coherent knowledge enhancement in current language programs is particularly damaging for disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: This missed opportunity is particularly fateful for disadvantaged children, who are already far behind their advantaged classmates in general and verbal knowledge.

claims.text: The theory that expert readers bypass sounding out words to process written text through a separate speedy process is incorrect.
claims.source_passage: It was claimed that expert readers process the written word through a speedy, separate process that short-circuits the time-consuming activity of sounding out words. We now know that this theory is incorrect.

claims.text: The way humans process written words and spoken words is essentially the same, utilizing a 'phonological loop'.
claims.source_passage: The way we process the written word and the way we process the spoken word are essentially the same. Readers must go through what researchers call “the phonological loop,” which informs our reading and speaking as well as our thinking.

claims.text: A child's ability to understand a text being read aloud usually sets the limit for their ability to understand that same text when reading it independently.
claims.source_passage: It is intuitively obvious that any limit on children’s ability to understand a text that is being read aloud to them usually limits their ability to understand that same text when they read it by themselves.

claims.text: Modern systematic phonics programs are generally admirable but fail to include systematic expansion of background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The systematic phonics in these programs (now, on the whole, admirable) are not backed up by a systematic approach to the background knowledge that children will need for later reading comprehension.

claims.text: The phonological loop informs reading, speaking, and thinking.
claims.source_passage: what researchers call “the phonological loop,” which informs our reading and speaking as well as our thinking.

claims.text: Progress in reading and progress in language comprehension are not separate processes.
claims.source_passage: few people would openly defend the idea that progress in reading and in language comprehension are separate.

claims.text: Early reading programs neglect the systematic expansion of children's general knowledge and vocabulary due to a formalistic view of comprehension.
claims.source_passage: early reading materials and programs... take such a formalistic view of reading comprehension that they neglect the systematic expansion of children’s general knowledge and accompanying vocabulary.

claims.text: Systematic phonics programs currently lack a necessary accompanying systematic approach to background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The systematic phonics in these programs (now, on the whole, admirable) are not backed up by a systematic approach to the background knowledge that children will need for later reading comprehension.

claims.text: Listening ability in grade two reliably forecasts reading ability in grade five.
claims.source_passage: But we have known since 1974 (thanks to the work of Thomas Sticht) that listening ability in grade two reliably forecasts reading ability in grade five.

claims.text: Proficient reading and proficient listening both depend on a general ability to comprehend language, independent of the medium.
claims.source_passage: proficient reading and proficient listening both depend on an ability to comprehend language, quite apart from whether the language is expressed orally or in writing.

claims.text: Gains in oral speech, knowledge, and vocabulary made in kindergarten or first grade are gains in reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Every gain in oral speech, in knowledge and in vocabulary and in the conventions of formal discourse, that children make in kindergarten or first grade is ultimately a gain in reading comprehension, whether or not that fact is immediately evident.

claims.text: Holding children's progress in language and knowledge hostage to their progress in decoding unnecessarily retards their reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: If children’s progress in language and knowledge is held hostage to their progress in decoding, their ability to read with comprehension will be unnecessarily retarded.

claims.text: Early grades should place heavy emphasis on nonwritten, oral activities like reading aloud and discussing coherent, challenging material.
claims.source_passage: It is precisely for the sake of reading and writing that we need to place a great deal of emphasis in the early grades on nonwritten, oral activities—on adults reading aloud coherent and challenging material, on discussing it, on having children elaborate on these materials.

claims.text: Children must become familiar with the public genres of oral speech to succeed in reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: it is essential for young children to become familiar with the more public genres of oral speech, which are the main genres of reading and writing.

claims.text: Conversational language is usually informal, while written language is 'elaborated.'
claims.source_passage: there are some practical differences between conversational language, which is usually informal in nature, and written language, which has to be elaborated.

claims.text: Radio and television speech is functionally similar to reading aloud to a large, diverse audience.
claims.source_passage: Radio and television are like reading aloud to a large, diverse audience.

claims.text: Radio talk and writing both require addressing an unknown, unseen audience using specific formal conventions.
claims.source_passage: Radio talk and writing have the same task to perform. Both are addressed to an unknown, unseen audience.

claims.text: Reading and listening to public discourse require sharing the background knowledge the author or speaker takes for granted.
claims.source_passage: the listener or reader must know the background knowledge that the speaker or writer is taking for granted.

claims.text: Learning to read and write is intrinsically tied to learning how to speak and listen to strangers.
claims.source_passage: Learning how to read and write is very much tied up with learning how to listen and speak to strangers.

claims.text: Children usually enter school without having learned how to make themselves understood to unseen strangers.
claims.source_passage: children when they first come to school have not usually learned how to make themselves understood to unseen strangers.

claims.text: Oral speech delivered over the radio is highly similar to written speech.
claims.source_passage: The oral speech that takes place over the radio is so similar to written speech that it can be transcribed directly to make plausible sense in writing.

claims.text: Practicing pretend radio or audience-directed speeches helps children grasp the nature of reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: As a parent or teacher, we can help our children grasp what reading and writing are like by practicing pretend radio or other audiencedirected speeches.

claims.text: Teachers should frequently require children to make formal prepared and unprepared presentations to the class.
claims.source_passage: In the classroom, the teacher can and should ask children frequently to make formal prepared and unprepared presentations to the class.

claims.text: Making formal presentations is the best device for preparing young children to be good readers and writers because it enacts the communicative situation without the burden of decoding.
claims.source_passage: This is the best practice for becoming a good writer and reader that we can devise for young children, because it enacts the communicative situation of reading and writing without involving the arduous process of learning to sound out and form letters accurately and fluently.

claims.text: The long-range task of decoding should not hold back a student's progress in language and knowledge.
claims.source_passage: That decoding task is absolutely essential, but as we’ve seen, it is a different task, and it is a long-range one that should not hold back progress in language and knowledge.

claims.text: Learning to function in a speech community requires distinguishing between formal speech and ordinary conversation.
claims.source_passage: The distinction is an essential feature of learning to function in a speech community.

claims.text: Real conversations are often incomprehensible to listeners who lack awareness of the specific context.
claims.source_passage: Real conversations are often incomprehensible to listeners who happen to be unaware of the specific context in which the conversation occurs.

claims.text: Early reading programs neglect instruction in the public conventions of speaking and listening.
claims.source_passage: Early reading programs in the schools have paid too little attention to instruction in the special conventions and obligations that must constrain speakers and listeners in public situations.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a technical skill; it is the counterpart to knowing how to speak and write understandably to strangers.
claims.source_passage: Reading comprehension is not a technical skill that must be learned as though the text were a given that drifted down from the sky. It is the other side of knowing how to speak and write in an understandable way to strangers within a particular speech community.

claims.text: The skills of reading, writing, speaking, and listening are all culturally infused with the traditions of a specific group.
claims.source_passage: The skills of reading and writing, like the skills of speaking and listening, are infused with the traditions of a particular group.

claims.text: The refinement of clear English prose as a communication tool took approximately two centuries, from 1500 to 1700.
claims.source_passage: The perfection of written communication by means of a clear prose style took quite a long time. In English it took two centuries, from 1500 to 1700.

claims.text: Writing techniques are fundamentally the same as techniques used for speaking to large, heterogeneous groups.
claims.source_passage: while writing and printing led over time to the refinement of ever more efficient rhetorical techniques, these are fundamentally the same techniques that people have always adopted when they are speaking to large, heterogeneous groups.

claims.text: Reading and writing are not inherently different from listening and speaking based on any special characteristic of speech.
claims.source_passage: There is no special characteristic of speech that makes reading and writing inherently different from listening and speaking.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a technical skill that can be learned in isolation from the content of the text.
claims.source_passage: Reading comprehension is not a technical skill that must be learned as though the text were a given that drifted down from the sky.

claims.text: Reading and writing are the counterparts of speaking and writing to strangers within a specific speech community.
claims.source_passage: It is the other side of knowing how to speak and write in an understandable way to strangers within a particular speech community.

claims.text: There is no inherent psychological or functional difference between speech and reading/writing.
claims.source_passage: There is no special characteristic of speech that makes reading and writing inherently different from listening and speaking.

claims.text: All forms of language comprehension, whether auditory or visual, must pass through the phonological loop.
claims.source_passage: Psychologically, all of these must pass through the “phonological loop.”

claims.text: Language comprehension is primarily determined by background knowledge and familiarity with speech conventions.
claims.source_passage: What counts most from the standpoint of language comprehension is the kind of utterance that is being engaged in and whether the audience has the background knowledge, including knowledge of speech conventions, they need to comprehend it.

claims.text: Effective communication to large, heterogeneous audiences requires high levels of explicitness in syntax and background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Children need to learn that when they have to address a big, heterogeneous audience, such as a classroom full of peers, they have to be more explicit both in syntax and in overtly stated background knowledge.

claims.text: Neglecting formal oral language enhancement in the early grades has negatively impacted social justice.
claims.source_passage: In view of the inseparability of oral and written speech, neglecting intensive formal oral language enhancement during the long hours devoted to reading in the early grades has been more than a missed opportunity; it has been a disaster for social justice.

claims.text: The combination of oral language enhancement and systematic knowledge instruction is the key to closing the achievement gap.
claims.source_passage: Early oral language enhancement plus the systematic teaching of enabling knowledge are the keys to later gains in all academic areas, and also to narrowing the achievement gap between demographic groups.

claims.text: The Matthew effect in education, where the rich get richer in knowledge, originates in early language learning.
claims.source_passage: It is in early language learning that the Matthew effect begins to take hold.

claims.text: The common definition of 'reading' erroneously conflates decoding with comprehension.
claims.source_passage: But this vague combining of decoding and comprehension has caused confusion.

claims.text: Decoding fluency in grade four does not automatically develop into comprehension fluency in grade twelve.
claims.source_passage: decoding fluency in grade four, when our children’s scores are moderately high by international standards, does not automatically develop into comprehension fluency in grade twelve, when our students’ scores are low.

claims.text: Reading comprehension does not automatically improve through exposure to increasingly complex materials.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension is not a skill that automatically grows into an ability to cope with complex materials once a child has been given a start with simple ones.

claims.text: American students' reading scores are moderately high by international standards in grade four but low in grade twelve.
claims.source_passage: our children’s scores are moderately high by international standards, does not automatically develop into comprehension fluency in grade twelve, when our students’ scores are low.

claims.text: Decoding is a skill that can be successfully transferred from one text to another.
claims.source_passage: it is also true that decoding is a skill that can be transferred from one text to another.

claims.text: The progress of a child's reading comprehension does not follow a reliable or automatic course of development.
claims.source_passage: But the progress of a child’s reading comprehension is different. That progress does not follow a reliable course of development. Comprehension is not a skill that automatically grows into an ability to cope with complex materials once a child has been given a start with simple ones.

claims.text: General reading skill applies only to texts that are consciously directed to a general audience within a definite speech community.
claims.source_passage: As we will see, general reading skill applies only to texts that are consciously directed to a general audience within a definite speech community.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests are accurate measures of general reading proficiency provided they include a diversity of subjects.
claims.source_passage: Within that limitation, well-educated people can and do exhibit a general proficiency in reading comprehension, and we can indeed reliably measure that proficiency on reading tests, just as the test-makers claim.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests must include a diversity of subject matters to maintain validity and reliability.
claims.source_passage: a look at those standardized reading tests will disclose that they always include a diversity of passages on quite different subjects—a feature that is absolutely critical to their validity and reliability as tests.

claims.text: A reader who understands one subject well, such as the Civil War, will not necessarily understand an unrelated subject like molecular interactions.
claims.source_passage: Someone who reads well about the Civil War may not necessarily read well about molecular interactions.

claims.text: General reading comprehension requires a definite range of general knowledge rather than just vocabulary and strategies.
claims.source_passage: General reading comprehension ability is much more than comprehension strategies; it requires a definite range of general knowledge.

claims.text: Prior knowledge of a subject matter is a more powerful predictor of reading comprehension than high technical decoding and strategy skills.
claims.source_passage: the reading comprehension of the low-skills, baseball-knowing group proved superior to the reading comprehension of the high-skills, baseball-ignorant group for that particular text.

claims.text: The meaning of a text depends largely on what the text implies but does not explicitly state.
claims.source_passage: Researchers have discovered that what the text implies but doesn’t say is a necessary part of its understood meaning. In fact, what the text doesn’t say often far exceeds what it says.

claims.text: The skill of comprehension is fundamentally the skill of filling in the unsaid 'blanks' using relevant knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension skill cannot be automatically transferred from one text to another, because the skill of comprehension is basically the skill of filling in enough of what has been left unsaid—that is, filling in enough blanks—to make sense of the text.

claims.text: Richness of communication is achieved by taking shared knowledge for granted, which allows for speed and concentration.
claims.source_passage: The more a speaker can take for granted, the less he has to say explicitly and the more rapid and concentrated communication can become. Speed is important, because a person can keep in mind only a very few items at a time, which means that richness of meaning is gained by condensing ordinary oral speech.

claims.text: Written speech is generally more explicit than oral speech but still leaves a great deal unsaid.
claims.source_passage: Written speech is the same, only less so. It leaves less unspoken. It is more explicit, because writers know that they must make it so in order to be understood. But writing always leaves a great deal unsaid.

claims.text: When a text is being understood, the reader or listener fills in unstated connections to create an imagined situation model based on domain-specific knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Cognitive psychologists have determined that when a text is being understood, the reader (or listener) is filling in a lot of the unstated connections between the words to create an imagined situation model based on domain-specific knowledge.

claims.text: The constructed situation model constitutes the actual understood meaning of a text.
claims.source_passage: This constructed situation model constitutes the understood meaning of the text.

claims.text: Understanding simple phrases like 'North against South' requires a wealth of preexisting background information beyond basic vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: A wealth of preexisting background information is needed to understand that simple phrase—going far beyond the root idea of compass directions, which is simply the necessary first step.

claims.text: To understand language, we must construct a situation model consisting of meanings from explicit words and meanings inferred from background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: To understand language, whether spoken or written, we need to construct a situation model consisting of meanings construed from the explicit words of the text as well as meanings inferred or constructed from relevant background knowledge.

claims.text: Without relevant, unspoken background knowledge, text cannot be understood.
claims.source_passage: Without this relevant, unspoken background knowledge, we can’t understand the text.

claims.text: Relevant, domain-specific knowledge is an absolute requirement for reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Since relevant, domain-specific knowledge is an absolute requirement for reading comprehension, there is no way around the need for children to gain broad general knowledge in order to gain broad general proficiency in reading.

claims.text: The American educational community believes that no specific background knowledge is needed for reading and that any general knowledge will suffice.
claims.source_passage: According to received views in the American educational community, no specific background knowledge is needed for reading. Any general background knowledge will serve.

claims.text: The idea that any background knowledge will serve frees schools from the requirement to teach a specific body of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: This innocent-sounding idea, so liberating to the teacher and the student, frees the American school from any requirement to teach a specific body of knowledge.

claims.text: American reading proficiency and verbal SAT scores have declined drastically over the last half-century.
claims.source_passage: during which time, as we’ve seen, American reading proficiency and verbal SAT scores have declined drastically.

claims.text: Television and social forces cannot fully explain the decline in American reading proficiency.
claims.source_passage: Scapegoats for the decline, such as television and social forces, have been invoked to explain it, but they cannot fully explain why other nations, equally addicted to television but not to American educational theories that disparage “mere” information, have not suffered a similarly drastic decline in reading proficiency.

claims.text: The educational community prioritizes teaching comprehension strategies to help children use their existing individual world knowledge rather than teaching a shared body of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The goal of reading instruction is primarily to make the child ever more fluent in using comprehension strategies to ferret out meaning.

claims.text: The goal of modern reading instruction is primarily to make children fluent in using comprehension strategies to ferret out meaning.
claims.source_passage: The goal of reading instruction is primarily to make the child ever more fluent in using comprehension strategies to ferret out meaning.

claims.text: All readers bring a growing knowledge of language, the world, and print usage to the act of reading.
claims.source_passage: All readers bring to the reading/writing process their own growing knowledge of language, the world, and their understandings of how print is used to convey meaning.

claims.text: A child's home environment and exposure to adults reading shapes their expectations and understandings of literacy.
claims.source_passage: A child who is often read to, or who regularly sees adults reading and writing for personal tasks and pleasure, will expect that reading and writing play useful roles in life and are valued activities.

claims.text: Effective teaching should foster expectations of reading as purposeful and build on learners' diverse areas of existing knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Effective teaching fosters these expectations of reading and writing as purposeful and meaningful acts, and honors and builds on learners’ diverse areas of knowledge through thoughtful selection of reading materials and activities.

claims.text: Academic skills should be built up from the knowledge that a child already possesses and finds interesting.
claims.source_passage: Academic skills should be built up from the knowledge that the child already has as well as from the knowledge that is most interesting and appropriate to the child.

claims.text: Research has demonstrated that a specific body of background knowledge is necessary for reading proficiency.
claims.source_passage: It is highly inconvenient to this doctrine that research has shown a body of specific background knowledge to be necessary for reading proficiency

claims.text: Specific background knowledge required for reading proficiency should be explicitly taught in school.
claims.source_passage: therefore it should be taught in school.

claims.text: The dominant educational view holds that the content of schooling should be left entirely to the individual locality, teacher, or student.
claims.source_passage: This runs up against the dominant view that the content of schooling should be left up entirely to the individual locality, the individual teacher, or the needs of the individual student

claims.text: The dominant educational view considers the content of schooling to be far less important than the learning of formal skills like reading and critical thinking.
claims.source_passage: the content of schooling is in itself far less important than the learning of formal skills such as ‘reading’ and ‘critical thinking.’

claims.text: Many children lack the general background knowledge necessary to understand a wide diversity of writings.
claims.source_passage: given the fact that many children lack the general background knowledge they need to understand a diversity of writings.

claims.text: Current educational programs are fragmented and trivial in their content.
claims.source_passage: How current programs, fragmented and trivial in content, could effectively provide the general knowledge that children require in order to gain proficiency in reading is not explained by proponents of the status quo.

claims.text: Proponents of the educational status quo believe that any diverse set of readings will automatically supply the necessary knowledge children need.
claims.source_passage: the status quo asserts that any reasonably diverse set of readings will, by its very diversity, supply the kinds of knowledge and experiences children need.

claims.text: A vague concept of curriculum content masks what is actually a formalistic conception of reading.
claims.source_passage: The alert reader will notice that a vague, unspecific concept of the content called for in a reading program masks what is ultimately a formalistic conception of reading.

claims.text: Early reading programs typically offer children fragmented readings that lack sustained information or coherence.
claims.source_passage: The practical result is that early reading programs offer the child a fragmented series of readings with little sustained information or coherence.

claims.text: Indifference to specific, cumulative subject matter is the primary reason reading comprehension has failed to improve.
claims.source_passage: I believe that this indifference to specific, cumulative subject matter, more than any other single trait of reading programs, has prevented them from significantly improving reading comprehension.

claims.text: Cognitive science has rejected a formalistic conception of language comprehension for many years.
claims.source_passage: For many years now, as we’ve seen, cognitive science has rejected a formalistic conception of language comprehension.

claims.text: Relevant background knowledge must be domain-specific to allow a reader to form a situation model.
claims.source_passage: We now know that the relevant background knowledge needed for reading comprehension must be domain-specific in order to enable the reader to form an adequate situation model.

claims.text: Verbal comprehension essentially consists of forming a mental situation model.
claims.source_passage: The finding that verbal comprehension consists of forming a situation model is a powerful and clarifying idea that is the fruit of a half-century of work in psycholinguistics.

claims.text: Constructing an accurate situation model for the Pledge of Allegiance requires specific knowledge of nations, flags, and the nature of solemn contracts.
claims.source_passage: All of these things are part of the situation model that the children must construct for themselves to understand the pledge, and because these things are unstated, they must construct the situation model from relevant knowledge that they already have.

claims.text: Formalistic theories of reading are popular because they allow educators to avoid the difficult intellectual and political task of choosing specific content.
claims.source_passage: If formalistic theories of reading were actually true, it would be a great convenience for everyone. Controversy could be avoided and reading scores would rise. No one would have to make hard decisions about what to teach and what order it should be taught in.

claims.text: Certain subjects are essential for education because they are inherently necessary for communication within a speech community.
claims.source_passage: But the debate will be more productive if its participants understand that certain subjects are essential by virtue of their inherent necessity for communication through speech and writing.

claims.text: Enabling shared knowledge should be protected from ideological and political controversy.
claims.source_passage: This enabling shared knowledge ought to be kept safe from ideological and political controversy, for if there is indeed knowledge that all children must have if they are to become proficient readers in our speech community, then it is our duty to determine what that knowledge is and show why it is needed.

claims.text: The content of enabling shared knowledge for a society is determined by its shared history.
claims.source_passage: An ever-changing shared history determines what the enabling, shared knowledge is for any society.

claims.text: Communication with strangers requires a speaker to accurately estimate what can be taken for granted versus what must be explained.
claims.source_passage: To assure communication when I address a stranger, I have to estimate what I can assume and what I must explain. Hence, my knowledge of what I can take for granted in communicating with strangers is critical to my successful membership in a speech community.

claims.text: Schools should teach the specific content that writers take for granted and do not explain.
claims.source_passage: We don’t need to teach them the things that writers directly explain; we need to teach them what writers take for granted and do not explain.

claims.text: If reading is recognized as content-dependent, process-oriented school practices must be repudiated.
claims.source_passage: When it becomes widely accepted that, on the contrary, reading is not a formal skill, and that reading proficiency requires a definable range of specific background knowledge, then we will have to repudiate a whole set of process-oriented, content-indifferent practices in the schools.

claims.text: American educational theory is transfixed by the idea of all-purpose how-to strategies such as critical thinking and inferencing.
claims.source_passage: I have observed that American educational theory has been transfixed by the idea of all-purpose how-to strategies, such as “critical thinking” and “inferencing”

claims.text: Teaching formal reading strategies for nine consecutive years (K-8) is a misguided activity and a waste of time.
claims.source_passage: So much time is being wasted on these misguided activities throughout the nation that if this book manages to persuade even a few teachers and administrators, it will have justified its existence.

claims.text: Time spent on formal reading strategies would be better spent teaching useful background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: young students were being subjected to formal reading strategies in an unsuccessful attempt to make them proficient readers when the time would be better spent teaching useful background knowledge.

claims.text: Existing reading programs emphasize teaching conscious formal processes to children continuously from kindergarten through eighth grade.
claims.source_passage: Kate Walsh, in an analysis of existing reading programs, has found that they continually emphasize teaching these conscious formal processes to children from kindergarten through eighth grade, year after year for nine years

claims.text: Exercises like 'looking for the main idea' are often conducted using stories with vacuous or useless content.
claims.source_passage: One of the favorite exercises is “looking for the main idea.” That the idea may be vacuous or useless is apparently less important in these programs than practicing the skill.

claims.text: Children are not less intelligent than adults; they are simply less well-informed.
claims.source_passage: But children are not less intelligent than adults, they are just less well informed, and if an adult finds this story empty and pointless, so will a child.

claims.text: Reading experts prioritize metacognitive skills over the substance and coherence of the text.
claims.source_passage: we are assured by reading experts that the substance of the stories is far less important than the teaching of “metacognitive skills” such as clarifying, “inferencing,” and questioning.

claims.text: Expert readers perform comprehension tasks like finding the main idea automatically and without conscious attention.
claims.source_passage: It is true that expert readers do show an ability to find the main idea and to make inferences that aren’t in the passage, and, of course, expert readers do these things automatically, without consciously attending to the processes.

claims.text: Consciously attending to self-monitoring skills during reading reduces expertise by consuming attentional space.
claims.source_passage: If they did consciously attend to such self-monitoring skills, as the young child is being asked to do, they would instantly become less expert in their task, for paying conscious attention to such processes leaves less attentional space for thinking about the implications of the text being read.

claims.text: Metacognitive strategies are not abstract, transferable abilities that can be detached from substantive knowledge of the subject matter.
claims.source_passage: There is little scientific reason to expect ... that the “metacognitive strategies” used by experts are abstract, transferable abilities that can be detached from substantive knowledge of the subject matter of the text.

claims.text: Strategy-based reading methods currently used in American schools do not work to produce proficient readers.
claims.source_passage: We know from large-scale studies that these now universally applied methods do not work.

claims.text: Increasing the number of strategy lessons beyond a minimal amount (e.g., six lessons) has no significant effect on reading proficiency.
claims.source_passage: Twenty-five lessons in reading strategies have as little effect as six lessons.

claims.text: Young children demonstrate expert-level reading skills (inferencing, summarizing) when reading about a subject they already know well.
claims.source_passage: Young children who are normally very inexpert readers will, when confronted with a subject they know something about, manage to show an unaccustomed ability to find the main idea, to make inferences, to classify, and to remember—and all of this to a degree that exceeds the skill of the normally “expert” reader who lacks intimate subject-matter knowledge.

claims.text: Inference is arguably the most important component of reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, of all the comprehension strategies being taught as formal skills, “inferencing” (the jargon term for inferring) is arguably the most important, for inference is the essence of comprehension, as cognitive scientists have amply shown.

claims.text: Making an inference is the act of supplying missing substance from background knowledge, not a formal operation.
claims.source_passage: But filling in a blank is not a formal skill; it is not supplying a missing formal operation. It is supplying a missing substance.

claims.text: Inferences are made not because of a special 'inferencing' skill but because the reader knows something relevant about the subject that is unstated in the text.
claims.source_passage: We are able to make it not because we have some special “inferencing” skill but because we know something relevant about Socrates that is unstated in the text.

claims.text: Inference is the act of filling in a blank in the text with substance (knowledge) rather than performing a formal operation.
claims.source_passage: But filling in a blank is not a formal skill; it is not supplying a missing formal operation. It is supplying a missing substance.

claims.text: Encouraging 'why' questions (questioning the author) is a feature of ordinary subject-matter teaching rather than a specialized reading strategy instruction.
claims.source_passage: That result can just as readily be interpreted as a defense of ordinary subject-matter teaching, since encouraging “why” questions is the sort of instruction most good teachers use to help students understand and think about something they are reading.

claims.text: It is a failed educational principle to prioritize learning formal procedures over the actual content being read.
claims.source_passage: I disagree only with the practice of applying this age-old strategy to a series of readings with little cumulative educational value on the principle that learning formal procedures is more important for future reading competency than the actual content being read.

claims.text: Strategy exercises provide a small initial benefit to young readers by helping them realize that text is a form of communication intended to convey meaning.
claims.source_passage: For very inexperienced children, explicit practice in “finding the main idea” does help at first, because such exercises help them understand that reading a text is like listening to somebody speak to them.

claims.text: The benefits of 'finding the main idea' strategy instruction level off quickly once the child understands the communicative nature of text.
claims.source_passage: But this initial benefit in consciously “finding the main idea” quickly levels off. Once children get the idea that a text is like somebody trying to tell them something, they don’t have to relearn the principle time after time.

claims.text: Spending more than approximately six sessions on strategy instruction is a waste of instructional time.
claims.source_passage: But spending more than about six sessions on strategies not only wastes valuable time but actually gets in the way of reading comprehension

claims.text: Self-consciously applying strategies consumes attentional space and reduces the mental resources available for actual meaning construction.
claims.source_passage: the effort a student devotes to self-consciously finding the main idea is activity that occupies attentional space and subtracts from the child’s mental resources available to construct meaning.

claims.text: Children's oral comprehension strategies, including inferencing, are already highly developed before they enter school.
claims.source_passage: Young children do not have to be taught the basics of “inferencing” and other comprehension strategies. Their oral comprehension strategies are already well developed before they come to school.

claims.text: The successful acquisition of a mother tongue is decisive proof that children possess sophisticated inferencing skills.
claims.source_passage: Children have already learned an oral language. The initial process of learning the mother tongue requires each of us to exercise “inferencing skills” that are as demanding as any inferences we will make during the rest of our lives.

claims.text: First-grade students are capable of understanding complex inferential language such as irony and sarcasm.
claims.source_passage: That is why most first-graders are able to understand even such highly inferential speech conventions as irony and sarcasm (“Man, that’s bad”)

claims.text: What young children lack in reading comprehension is not strategy, but knowledge of formal language conventions and knowledge of the world.
claims.source_passage: It is not mainly comprehension strategies that young children lack in comprehending texts but knowledge—knowledge of formal language conventions and knowledge of the world.

claims.text: The transition away from the 'romantic' view of decoding was spearheaded by specific figures including Marilyn Jager Adams, Jeanne Chall, Reid Lyon, and Elizabeth McPike.
claims.source_passage: First-rate scientists like Marilyn Jager Adams and Jeanne Chall... encouraged by the farsighted government administrator Reid Lyon and by the crusading editor... Elizabeth McPike, have turned the tide against the romantic notion that decoding is best regarded as a natural process.

claims.text: Marion Joseph's advocacy was responsible for shifting not only the California school system but also the textbook publishers toward systematic and explicit alphabetic instruction.
claims.source_passage: Marion Joseph has been credited with turning the entire school system of California (and the publishers who sell books to California) toward the systematic and explicit teaching of the alphabetic/phonemic system of written English.

claims.text: Admittedly, the idea that instruction should build on a child's existing individual world knowledge has some validity as a starting point.
claims.source_passage: As with most American educational doctrines, the notion that we must start with the child’s individual knowledge has some validity. The background knowledge that a child brings to bear in trying to comprehend a text obviously has to be the knowledge she has already acquired before beginning to read.

claims.text: The fact that other nations with high television consumption have not seen a decline in reading proficiency suggests that American educational theories, rather than social forces like TV, are responsible for the US decline.
claims.source_passage: Scapegoats for the decline, such as television and social forces, have been invoked to explain it, but they cannot fully explain why other nations, equally addicted to television but not to American educational theories that disparage 'mere' information, have not suffered a similarly drastic decline in reading proficiency.

claims.text: According to Chief Justice John Marshall, the U.S. Constitution must rely on extensive taken-for-granted knowledge because detailing every subdivision of power would make it too 'prolix' for the human mind to embrace.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, one of Chief Justice John Marshall’s famous comments about the U.S. Constitution was that 'to contain an accurate detail of all the subdivisions of which its great powers will admit... would partake of the prolixity of a legal code and could scarcely be embraced by the human mind.'

claims.text: Spending excessive time on reading strategies is counterproductive because the conscious effort required to apply them subtracts from the mental resources needed to actually construct meaning.
claims.source_passage: spending more than about six sessions on strategies not only wastes valuable time but actually gets in the way of reading comprehension, since... the effort a student devotes to self-consciously finding the main idea is activity that occupies attentional space and subtracts from the child’s mental resources available to construct meaning.

claims.text: The refinement of clear English prose style between 1500 and 1700 was essentially the development of techniques for addressing large, heterogeneous groups.
claims.source_passage: The perfection of written communication by means of a clear prose style took quite a long time. In English it took two centuries, from 1500 to 1700... these are fundamentally the same techniques that people have always adopted when they are speaking to large, heterogeneous groups.

evidence.description: The research consensus and 'voluminous' empirical evidence support explicit decoding instruction over 'natural' methods.
Pathbreaking research demonstrating the necessity of systematic phonics instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Jeanne Chall, Learning to Read: The Great Debate (1965)

evidence.description: Improvements in decoding ability across California following a shift toward systematic teaching of the alphabetic system.
Reading specialist estimates regarding the vocabulary threshold (90%) required for context-based learning.
Scientific research identifying the 'phonological loop' as the mechanism for processing both reading and speaking.
The observation of the gap in general and verbal knowledge between disadvantaged and advantaged children.
The logical observation that children's listening comprehension level effectively caps their potential reading comprehension level.
Correlational study showing that a child's listening comprehension in 2nd grade is a reliable predictor of their reading comprehension in 5th grade.
evidence.source_passage: But we have known since 1974 (thanks to the work of Thomas Sticht) that listening ability in grade two reliably forecasts reading ability in grade five.

evidence.description: Analysis of a transcript from NPR's Morning Edition showing that professional oral speech is structured similarly to written speech.
evidence.source_passage: The oral speech that takes place over the radio is so similar to written speech that it can be transcribed directly to make plausible sense in writing.

evidence.description: A transcript of an NPR interview between Steve Inskeep and John Feinstein about tennis which demonstrates how formal oral speech can be directly transcribed into coherent writing.
Transcripts of the Nixon tapes showing that ordinary, non-public conversation is often incoherent when transcribed and lacks the context necessary for a 'stranger' to understand it.
The historical timeframe (1500-1700) during which English prose style was refined into a tool for efficient written communication.
Historical timeline of the development of clear English prose from 1500 to 1700.
The transcript of a radio conversation between Steve Inskeep and John Feinman demonstrates that comprehension depends on 'tennis scene' knowledge rather than unique reading skills.
National reading scores show that U.S. students perform well in grade 4 (focus on decoding) but poorly in grade 12 (focus on comprehension).
National and international reading score data showing a decline in relative US performance between grade four and grade twelve.
Standardized test design practices requiring diverse subject matter passages to ensure validity.
An 'elegant experiment' (The Baseball Study) comparing students with high/low reading skills against high/low subject knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Recht and Leslie (1988), implied by endnote 18

evidence.description: The concept of the 'enthymeme' in classical rhetoric used to describe logical gaps filled by the audience.
Cognitive psychology research on the construction of situation models during text processing.
Analysis of a Civil War passage for second graders showing the density of required background knowledge for simple terms.
Historical trend data showing a 50-year decline in American verbal SAT scores.
Comparison between the US and other nations with similar television usage but different educational theories regarding reading decline.
A statement on background knowledge from the Bank Street School of Education emphasizing individual knowledge over specific content.
A 'half-century of work in psycholinguistics' regarding verbal comprehension and situation models.
The example of children reciting the Pledge of Allegiance and the background knowledge required to understand it.
The unstated assumptions required to understand the Pledge of Allegiance (concept of flag, nation, solemnity).
Chief Justice John Marshall’s comment on the U.S. Constitution regarding the impossibility of making every detail explicit.
evidence.source_passage: to contain an accurate detail of all the subdivisions of which its great powers will admit... would partake of the prolixity of a legal code and could scarcely be embraced by the human mind.

evidence.description: Linda Perlstein’s account of a school where formal strategy instruction failed to produce proficiency.
Kate Walsh's analysis of reading programs showing repetitive strategy instruction from K through 8th grade.
evidence.source_passage: Kate Walsh... has found that they continually emphasize teaching these conscious formal processes to children from kindergarten through eighth grade, year after year for nine years, classifying, drawing conclusions, making inferences, and predicting outcomes.

evidence.description: Analysis of existing reading programs showing a 9-year repetition of formal process instruction from K-8.
An example of a first-grade story used for strategy instruction that is judged as empty and pointless.
evidence.source_reference: What’s New in Mrs. Powell’s Class? / Mr. Brown’s Farm

evidence.description: Large-scale studies showing that 25 lessons of reading strategies are no more effective than six.
The Socrates syllogism used to demonstrate that logical inference requires specific factual premises (Socrates is a man) rather than just a formal skill.
The phenomenon of expert golfers 'clutching' or playing worse when they consciously attend to the mechanics of their swing.
The successful learning of a native language serves as proof of high-level inferencing capability in young children.
The observation that first-graders in both suburban and inner-city environments understand irony and sarcasm, such as the phrase 'Man, that's bad.'
Cognitive psychology research suggesting that conscious strategy use occupies finite attentional resources.

concepts.author_definition: The process of turning printed symbols into sounds and words quickly and accurately.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes conflated with the entirety of 'reading' itself.
concepts.source_passage: good first-step readers who can turn printed symbols into sounds and words quickly and accurately, a process called decoding.

concepts.author_definition: A phenomenon in which initial advantages in knowledge lead to greater cumulative gains, while initial deficits lead to increasing relative failure.
concepts.source_passage: reading researchers have spoken of the “Matthew effect” in reading. Those who already have good language understanding will gain still more language proficiency, while those who lack initial understanding will fall further and further behind.

concepts.author_definition: The actual subject matter or concrete knowledge of the world that words symbolize, necessary for a reader to make sense of a text.
concepts.source_passage: it’s also the kind of reality that the words are referring to. When a child doesn’t understand those word meanings and those referred-to realities, being good at sounding out words is a dead end.

concepts.author_definition: The cognitive process through which we process written words, spoken words, and thoughts, involving sounding out internal language.
The thesis that reading comprehension deficits are primarily caused by gaps in background knowledge rather than a lack of transferable 'reading skills'.
The incorrect belief that reading is a distinct developmental process that evolves independently from general language and oral knowledge.
A formal and explicit use of language required for communicating with an unseen audience (writing or broadcasting), distinct from informal conversational language used with intimates.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes referred to as 'academic language' or 'literary language.'
concepts.source_passage: written language, which has to be elaborated.

concepts.author_definition: Oral speech addressed to strangers or an unknown audience (like radio talk) which follows the same formal conventions as written language.
concepts.source_passage: it is essential for young children to become familiar with the more public genres of oral speech, which are the main genres of reading and writing.

concepts.author_definition: The ability to communicate explicitly and formally to an audience that does not share the speaker's immediate physical or situational context; the foundation of both formal speech and writing.
Context-dependent speech that relies on shared immediate surroundings and is often incomprehensible to those outside the immediate interaction.
A group within which individuals function by adhering to shared linguistic traditions, conventions, and obligations for public and private communication.
The ability to understand a wide diversity of texts directed to a general audience within a definite speech community, predicated on possessing general knowledge.
A term from Aristotle describing a syllogism with logically necessary steps left out, used here as a metaphor for the 'blanks' readers must fill in to comprehend text.
An imagined mental representation of the connections and situation described in a text, constructed by the reader using domain-specific knowledge.
Prior knowledge about the specific subject matter of a text, which allows a reader to fill in implications and construct meaning.

concepts.term: Received Views in the American Educational Community
concepts.author_definition: The prevailing educational ideology which holds that any background knowledge is equivalent and that schools need not teach a specific, cumulative curriculum.
The view that reading is primarily the mastery of formal, content-independent skills and strategies rather than the acquisition of specific knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Reading is an abstract skill that can be applied to any text regardless of subject matter.
concepts.source_passage: masks what is ultimately a formalistic conception of reading. The status quo asserts that offering students different kinds of writings will automatically prepare them to read different kinds of texts, whether or not the chosen selections are designed to foster the specific knowledge that is most enabling for future reading.

concepts.author_definition: The theory that reading is a set of content-indifferent, general-purpose skills or strategies (like inferencing) that can be applied to any text regardless of background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The idea that reading is primarily about decoding and 'how-to' strategies.

concepts.author_definition: The body of taken-for-granted information that members of a speech community assume their audience knows, which is necessary for successful communication.
Self-monitoring processes such as clarifying, inferencing, and questioning that are supposed to lead to reading expertise.
concepts.common_alternative: General cognitive skills that improve learning efficiency regardless of content.
concepts.source_passage: These “self-monitoring” skills are supposed to lead more rapidly to reading expertise.

concepts.author_definition: The act of filling in a blank in a text by supplying missing substance from background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A formal logical or linguistic skill that can be practiced on any text.
concepts.source_passage: Inference is filling in a blank in the text that we need to fill in to understand it. But filling in a blank is not a formal skill; it is not supplying a missing formal operation. It is supplying a missing substance.

concepts.author_definition: The limited cognitive capacity available for processing the implications of a text, which is depleted by conscious focus on reading strategies.
The act of filling in a 'blank' in a text by supplying missing substance/knowledge, rather than performing a formal skill or operation.
Brief exercises (optimally around six sessions) used to help children transfer pre-existing oral comprehension skills to the act of reading.
Widespread misconceptions about reading that must be cleared away before meaningful reform can occur.
The excessive detail that results when a speaker or writer fails to rely on shared, taken-for-granted knowledge, making a text too long and complex to comprehend.
The specific pieces of knowledge and referred-to realities that allow a child to understand a text once decoding is mastered.

cases.name: California's phonics reform
cases.description: In the decade prior to publication, activist Marion Joseph influenced the California school system and textbook publishers to move away from 'natural' decoding methods toward systematic and explicit teaching of the alphabetic/phonemic system. This led to visible improvements in children's ability to sound out words.
key_passages: Marion Joseph has been credited with turning the entire school system of California (and the publishers who sell books to California) toward the systematic and explicit teaching of the alphabetic/phonemic system of written English. The resulting improvements in first-step reading are already apparent.

cases.name: American Early Language Neglect
cases.description: In recent decades, the U.S. school system has prioritized simple written texts and decoding mechanics while neglecting the oral language and background knowledge development of students aged 4-7.
key_passages: In recent decades... there has been a relative neglect of enhancing oral language in the early years of schooling (ages four through seven). All across America, such neglect has proved fateful for children’s reading in later years.

cases.name: NPR Morning Edition Transcript
cases.description: Hirsch provides a transcribed segment of a National Public Radio interview between Steve Inskeep and John Feinstein regarding Roger Federer and Maria Sharapova's Wimbledon victories. He uses this to demonstrate that 'radio talk' is structured like written text and requires specific background knowledge (Wimbledon, tennis seeds, players) to be understood by an unseen audience.
key_passages: STEVE INSKEEP (host): What may have been the best weekend of tennis in years ended yesterday. Roger Federer won his second straight Wimbledon men’s singles title. The day before, Maria Sharapova won the women’s title. The teenager stunned the two-time defending champion, Serena Williams. Commentator John Feinstein joins us now. Good morning, John.

cases.name: The Nixon Tapes
cases.description: Secretly recorded conversations of President Nixon and his staff. Hirsch uses these to illustrate that even among highly educated adults, 'real' conversation is fragmented and context-dependent, making it nearly impossible for a stranger to understand via transcript.
key_passages: This was very evident in the famous Nixon tapes, in which actual conversations were secretly tape-recorded and then transcribed.
The transcript makes little coherent sense as a piece of writing, though, because it is not consciously addressed to others

cases.name: NPR Morning Edition Interview
cases.description: An interview regarding a tennis match between Roger Federer and Andy Roddick. Hirsch provides this as an example of 'radio talk'—oral speech that is structured for an audience and therefore functions exactly like written text.
key_passages: The oral speech that takes place over the radio is so similar to written speech that it can be transcribed directly to make plausible sense in writing.
This conversation makes perfect sense as a piece of writing, because it does just what writing has to do—it addresses itself to strangers.

cases.name: The development of English prose (1500-1700)
cases.description: The author notes that it took two centuries for English to refine a clear prose style suitable for efficient communication to wide audiences.
key_passages: In English it took two centuries, from 1500 to 1700.

cases.name: U.S. National Reading Achievement (Grade 4 vs Grade 12)
cases.description: A comparison of U.S. reading scores showing that students are internationally competitive in grade 4 but fall to low levels by grade 12 as the requirements shift from decoding to comprehension.
key_passages: decoding fluency in grade four, when our children’s scores are moderately high by international standards, does not automatically develop into comprehension fluency in grade twelve, when our students’ scores are low.

cases.name: The Baseball Study
cases.description: An experiment testing four groups of students on their comprehension of a baseball text. The study found that students with low technical reading skills but high baseball knowledge outperformed students with high technical skills but low baseball knowledge.
key_passages: In two of the four groups studied, one had good decoding skills but poor knowledge of the subject, baseball, while the poor-decoding group knew a lot about baseball. As predicted, the reading comprehension of the low-skills, baseball-knowing group proved superior to the reading comprehension of the high-skills, baseball-ignorant group for that particular text.

cases.name: Civil War Example Text
cases.description: A passage from Hirsch's own book 'What Your Second Grader Needs to Know' describing Generals Lee and Grant to illustrate the kind of text that requires background knowledge to process successfully.
key_passages: In 1861, the Civil War started. It lasted until 1865. It was American against American, North against South... General Robert E. Lee was in charge of the Southern army. General Ulysses S. Grant was in charge of the Northern army.

cases.name: The Civil War Passage for Second Graders
cases.description: An excerpt from Hirsch's book 'What Your Second Grader Needs to Know' used to illustrate that terms like 'North' and 'South' require inferences about geography, population, and political organization that aren't explicitly in the text.
key_passages: Take the phrase “North against South.” A wealth of preexisting background information is needed to understand that simple phrase—going far beyond the root idea of compass directions, which is simply the necessary first step.

cases.name: K-8 Reading Programs Analysis
cases.description: Kate Walsh analyzed existing American reading programs and found a massive redundancy in instruction. Programs focus on teaching the same formal processes (predicting, inferring, classifying) repeatedly from kindergarten through eighth grade, rather than building content knowledge.
key_passages: Kate Walsh, in an analysis of existing reading programs, has found that they continually emphasize teaching these conscious formal processes to children from kindergarten through eighth grade, year after year for nine years, classifying, drawing conclusions, making inferences, and predicting outcomes.

cases.name: Linda Perlstein’s observed school
cases.description: An account of a school where students were subjected to formal reading strategies in an attempt to make them proficient, which the author describes as unsuccessful and a waste of time compared to teaching background knowledge.
key_passages: using as an example Linda Perlstein’s account of a school where young students were being subjected to formal reading strategies in an unsuccessful attempt to make them proficient readers

thinkers.name: Jeanne Chall
thinkers.key_idea: Her research initiated the shift from seeing decoding as 'natural' to recognizing the need for explicit instruction.
thinkers.source_work: Learning to Read: The Great Debate (1965)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views her as a 'hero' whose research corrected a prevalent and harmful misconception about reading.
key_passages: First-rate scientists like Marilyn Jager Adams and Jeanne Chall, whose pathbreaking book... have turned the tide against the romantic notion that decoding is best regarded as a natural process.

thinkers.name: Marilyn Jager Adams
thinkers.key_idea: Decoding must be taught through explicit instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Cited as part of the scientific consensus that won the 'battle' for phonics.
key_passages: First-rate scientists like Marilyn Jager Adams... have shown that the best and fastest way to teach decoding is through persistent, explicit instruction.

thinkers.name: Marion Joseph
thinkers.key_idea: Advocacy for phonics and reading reform.
thinkers.engagement: Cited alongside Chall as a hero of previous educational battles whose work must now be extended into the realm of comprehension.
key_passages: need a new generation of heroes like Jeanne Chall and Marion Joseph to conduct the next phase of the battle

thinkers.name: Thomas Sticht
thinkers.key_idea: Listening ability in early grades is a reliable predictor of later reading ability.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Sticht's 1974 findings to debunk the 'wrong' assumption that language comprehension must wait for decoding fluency.
key_passages: But we have known since 1974 (thanks to the work of Thomas Sticht) that listening ability in grade two reliably forecasts reading ability in grade five.

thinkers.name: Steve Inskeep
thinkers.key_idea: Radio discourse that requires specific background knowledge (tennis) to understand.
thinkers.engagement: Uses a transcript of Inskeep's radio talk to show that comprehension is about knowledge of the subject matter, not 'reading skills.'
key_passages: To understand the transcript of Steve Inskeep and John Feinman, you don’t need special skills unique to reading; you need to know something about the tennis scene.

thinkers.name: Aristotle
thinkers.key_idea: Enthymeme — a syllogism with missing logical steps that the audience completes.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Aristotle's rhetorical concept to explain the cognitive process of reading, where readers must supply 'missing inferences' to make sense of a text.
key_passages: The ancient Greeks knew it, and Aristotle even gave the phenomenon a name—enthymeme, which is technically a syllogism with some of the logically necessary steps left out.

thinkers.name: Cognitive Psychologists
thinkers.key_idea: The discovery that understanding text requires the construction of a situation model via filling in unstated connections.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses this psychological finding as the scientific foundation for his argument that background knowledge is not optional for reading.
key_passages: Cognitive psychologists have determined that when a text is being understood, the reader (or listener) is filling in a lot of the unstated connections between the words to create an imagined situation model based on domain-specific knowledge.

thinkers.name: John Marshall
thinkers.key_idea: A constitution cannot explicitly detail every power and application without being too long for the human mind to grasp; it must rely on generalities that take specific details for granted.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Marshall's legal insight to illustrate the linguistic principle that all communication necessarily takes a vast amount of knowledge for granted.
key_passages: to contain an accurate detail of all the subdivisions of which its great powers will admit... would partake of the prolixity of a legal code and could scarcely be embraced by the human mind.

thinkers.name: Kate Walsh
thinkers.key_idea: Current reading programs are redundant and focus on formal processes over content for nearly a decade of schooling.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Walsh's empirical finding to support his claim that the current system wastes significant time on misguided 'how-to' strategies.
key_passages: Kate Walsh... has found that they continually emphasize teaching these conscious formal processes to children from kindergarten through eighth grade, year after year for nine years

thinkers.name: Linda Perlstein
thinkers.key_idea: Documented a school's unsuccessful use of formal reading strategies.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Perlstein's observations as an anecdotal example of his broader critique of educational theory.
key_passages: using as an example Linda Perlstein’s account of a school where young students were being subjected to formal reading strategies

thinkers.name: Reading researchers (unnamed)
thinkers.key_idea: Young readers require strategy instruction because they do not naturally know how to apply their existing background knowledge to texts.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch characterizes this group as being aware that knowledge is essential but wrongly concluding that the bottleneck is a lack of application skills rather than a lack of knowledge.
key_passages: Reading researchers who advocate the extensive teaching of comprehension strategies are aware of the finding that relevant background knowledge is essential to comprehension, but they take the position that this doesn’t diminish the desirability of strategy instruction, because, they say, young readers do not know how to apply the background knowledge they already have.

actors.name: Marion Joseph
actors.context: California activist who pushed for systematic phonics instruction.

actors.name: Elizabeth McPike
actors.context: Editor of The American Educator who advocated for research-based reading instruction.

actors.name: Reid Lyon
actors.context: Government administrator who supported the shift toward explicit decoding instruction.

actors.name: Jeanne Chall
actors.context: Harvard psychologist known for her work on the 'Reading Wars' and the stages of reading development.

actors.name: Marion Joseph
actors.context: California education reformer who successfully lobbied for phonics-based instruction.

actors.name: Thomas Sticht
actors.context: Educational researcher whose 1974 work linked listening and reading ability.

actors.name: Steve Inskeep
actors.context: NPR host used as an example of formal oral discourse.

actors.name: John Feinstein
actors.context: Sports commentator used in the NPR transcript example.

actors.name: Steve Inskeep
actors.context: NPR host in the tennis interview example.

actors.name: John Feinstein
actors.context: Sports commentator in the tennis interview example.

actors.name: Richard Nixon
actors.context: Subject of the recorded tapes used to show context-dependent speech.

actors.name: George Shultz
actors.context: Participant in the Nixon tape transcript.

actors.name: Steve Inskeep
actors.context: NPR host used as an example of oral language that requires background knowledge to comprehend.

actors.name: John Feinman
actors.context: Guest on NPR used as an example of technical/specialized speech.

actors.name: Test-makers
actors.context: Professional creators of standardized reading tests who discovered through experimentation that diverse subject matter is necessary for test validity.

actors.name: Cognitive Psychologists
actors.context: The scientific community that identifies the creation of situation models as the basis for text comprehension.

actors.name: Bank Street School of Education
actors.context: Used as a representative example of the American educational community's commitment to individualistic knowledge over a specific shared curriculum.

actors.name: American Educational Community

actors.name: Bank Street School of Education
actors.context: Cited as an example of the schools of education that promote the 'status quo' doctrines regarding background knowledge.

actors.name: Schools of Education
